titleStmt.title: Letter from May Fay to James Finn, 25 January 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: May Fay

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from May Fay (1897-1976) to her fiance, James (1877-1922). May writes that she has had a disgruntled suitor scolding her and discusses other local disagreements. She will be visiting Dublin once her mother returns from Mitchelstown.James Finn was a civil servant, working in the National Health Service Commission on Mount Street. May Fay became engaged to James Finn in January 1916. Despite the twenty year age gap their letters show a very personal and private insight into courting in early 20th century Ireland. While neither James nor May were politically active their letters provide a very unique insight into the affairs of Easter Week and its aftermath. James died in 1922 as a result of the flu epidemic, May was pregnant with their fourth child at the time. She held on to his letters as her reminder of him. May never remarried and focused her attention instead on her businesses in order to provide for their children

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Togherstown Ballinea 25 Jan 1916 My dearest James, Your letter this
morn was indeed a very pleasant
surprise I was not expecting a line
from you so soon, Yesterday
I wrote you a note saying I had
mumps I was frightened hearing
every one say they are so dangerous
for grown up people they are
nothing at all at least they
won't prevent my going to
town on Sat if Mother is
home by that time, Jennie
sent us a letter today saying
she was keeping her untill
Thursday that is alright if she
does not keep her longer you
know I am anxious for her return
for the one reason still I encourage
her to stay for Jennies sake
she is so far from home & Mother's
visits will I think be few & far between
so you will understand if we
dont get off as soon as we expected
but I am sure we wont be
later than Monday It is not
So long to wait tho' it seems
like a month since Mother
left. You say you have been
thinking of me for so long
that no doubt makes me feel
oh so flattered & happy still
it is hardly possible for me
to believe it since it is scarcely
a year since you came to know
me at all that is when I was by
this time last year a few months
before that about Oct I suppose
you do not remember we meet
in a tram you did not know
me and as well as I can remember
we did not speake, my fault
I suppose. I was with Jennie
& Jack. So now â I must
watch for Venus perhaps
this evening.
Mrs Seery has just been
here now with a letter to
your Mother for the post telling
her we will go on Sat so that
looks good for us. Jerome was down on Sun.
& in the even Mrs Jonnie & Ned
went to Balrath. The boys
I heard lost heavily
at the cards in a short time
Mrs S heard the Kilbeggan
bride diet read to a very
low degree she was very angry
with the dancers for putting
in their horse there disturbing
her father etc. I know you wont
feel hurt at the lack of friendship
there if you did I would not tell
you. Jack Nolan is out
there working to day he was
to dinner & when all were gone
he said ''corse you'd be thinking
yourself too good for a deacent
farmer as well as the rest.'
I said I was afraid I would
he shook his head & went out
he must have been making a
match for me he often tried that
on with Jennie you know. I fancy you have enough of this
so I will say goodbye with
warmest love but keeping the kisses
for a long time yet: thanks for you tho' Your ever loving May
